subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 9! In this unit you will grow into a confident adder and subtracter of three-digit numbers – all the numbers from 100 up to 999 and a little beyond. You already know how to add and subtract smaller numbers. Now you will learn careful, reliable methods for the bigger numbers you meet every day: the price of a goat at the Hargeisa market, the number of dates packed for iftar, the kilometres between Mogadishu and Kismaayo.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 3 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Add three-digit numbers using the column method, the expanded form method, and the number line method.
Subtract three-digit numbers using the same three methods.
Use regrouping (carrying) in addition and regrouping (borrowing) in subtraction confidently.
Use mental strategies – making tens, breaking apart, and compensation – for quicker sums.
Solve word problems that involve joining, separating, part-part-whole and comparison.
Solve multi-step problems that need more than one operation.
Check answers using estimation, inverse operations, and a different method.
Choose the best strategy for each addition or subtraction problem.

concepts.title: Adding Three-Digit Numbers with the Column Method
concepts.explanation: The column method is the most trusted way to add larger numbers. The idea is simple: write one number above the other so that ones sit under ones, tens under tens, and hundreds under hundreds. Then you add each column on its own, starting from the right. Keeping the columns straight is the whole secret – if the digits are lined up by place value, the method never lets you down. Let us add 347 + 285 together. First we stack them, lining up the place values, and add from right to left:
concepts.example: Here is the thinking, one column at a time. Ones: 7 + 5 = 12. That is more than 9, so we cannot fit it in one place. We write the 2 in the ones column and carry the 1 ten over to the top of the tens column. Tens: 1…

concepts.title: Adding with the Expanded Form and Number Line Methods
concepts.explanation: The column method is fast, but two other methods help you truly understand what is happening. They are worth knowing because they let you check your work in a different way. The expanded form method breaks each number into its hundreds, tens and ones, adds each place separately, then joins the parts back together. For 347 + 285 we write 347 = 300 + 40 + 7 and 285 = 200 + 80 + 5. Now add the matching parts: hundreds 300 + 200 = 500; tens 40 + 80 = 120; ones 7 + 5 = 12. Finally combine: 500 + 120 + 12 = 632. You get…
concepts.example: The number line method starts at one number – usually the larger – and jumps forward by the parts of the other number. For 347 + 285 we start at 347. Jump forward 200 to reach 547. Jump forward 80 to reach 627. Jump…

concepts.title: Adding with Regrouping (Carrying)
concepts.explanation: Regrouping in addition is also called carrying. You need it whenever a column adds up to 10 or more. Instead of trying to squeeze two digits into one place, you keep the ones digit of the total and carry the extra to the next column on the left. Look at 456 + 378. We stack and add from the right:
concepts.example: Ones: 6 + 8 = 14. Write the 4, carry 1 ten. Tens: 1 (carried) + 5 + 7 = 13. Write the 3, carry 1 hundred. Hundreds: 1 (carried) + 4 + 3 = 8. Write the 8. The answer is 834. Two of the three columns needed a carry here,…

concepts.title: Mental Addition Strategies
concepts.explanation: Not every sum needs to be written down. For smaller or friendlier numbers, you can add in your head using clever strategies. These make you quick and flexible, and they build a deep feel for how numbers work. Making tens adjusts a number to a friendly ten, then balances the change. For 47 + 28, push 47 up to 50 by adding 3, and pull 28 down to 25 by subtracting 3. The total is unchanged because you added 3 and took 3 away. Now 50 + 25 = 75. Rounding one number to a ten makes the sum much easier to hold in your…
concepts.example: Breaking apart numbers splits each number into tens and ones. For 47 + 28, break 47 into 40 + 7 and 28 into 20 + 8. Add the tens: 40 + 20 = 60. Add the ones: 7 + 8 = 15. Combine: 60 + 15 = 75. This is really the…

concepts.title: Subtracting Three-Digit Numbers with the Column Method
concepts.explanation: Subtraction with the column method works just like addition: line up the place values and work from the right. The difference is that when the top digit is smaller than the bottom digit, you cannot subtract yet – so you regroup (borrow) from the next column to the left. Let us work through 632 − 347. We stack the larger number on top:
concepts.example: Ones: 2 − 7 will not work, so we borrow 1 ten from the tens column. The 3 tens become 2 tens, and the 2 ones become 12 ones. Now 12 − 7 = 5. Tens: we now have 2 − 4, which will not work either, so we borrow 1 hundred.…

concepts.title: Subtracting with Expanded Form, Number Line, and Regrouping
concepts.explanation: Just like addition, subtraction can be done with the expanded form and number line methods, and these help you check the column answer. The expanded form method for 632 − 347 writes 632 = 600 + 30 + 2 and 347 = 300 + 40 + 7. Subtract hundreds: 600 − 300 = 300. Subtract tens: 30 − 40 will not work, so borrow 100 from the hundreds – the 300 becomes 200 and the 30 becomes 130; now 130 − 40 = 90. Subtract ones: 2 − 7 will not work, so borrow 10 from the tens – the 90 becomes 80 and the 2 becomes 12; now 12 − 7 = 5.…
concepts.example: The number line method for subtraction counts up from the smaller number to the larger, then adds the jumps. For 632 − 347 start at 347. Jump up to 400 (a jump of 53). Jump up to 600 (a jump of 200). Jump up to 632 (a…

methods.title: How to add with the column method
steps: Write one number above the other, lined up by place value.
Add the ones. If the total is 10 or more, write the ones digit and carry.
Add the tens, including any carried digit. Carry again if needed.
Add the hundreds, including any carried digit.

methods.title: How to subtract with the column method
steps: Write the larger number on top, lined up by place value.
Subtract the ones. If the top digit is too small, borrow from the tens.
Subtract the tens, borrowing from the hundreds if needed.
Subtract the hundreds.

methods.title: How to check an answer
steps: Estimate: round the numbers and check the size looks right.
Inverse: subtract to check an addition, or add to check a subtraction.
Different method: solve it again a second way and compare.

methods.title: Column subtraction with borrowing
steps: Solution: Ones: 5 − 9 will not work, borrow 1 ten – 4 tens become 3, 5 ones become 15; 15 − 9 = 6.
Tens: 3 − 5 will not work, borrow 1 hundred – 7 hundreds become 6, 3 tens become 13; 13 − 5 = 8.
Hundreds: 6 − 2 = 4.
The answer is 486.

methods.title: Subtraction by counting up (number line)
steps: Solution: Start at 287.
Jump to 300 (13).
Jump to 500 (200).
Jump to 563 (63).

methods.title: A comparison word problem
methods.example: Maryam is 143 cm tall. Her brother Idris is 178 cm tall. How much taller is Idris?
steps: Solution: This is a comparison, so we subtract: 178 − 143 = 35.
Idris is 35 cm taller.
Check the a comparison word problem result carefully.

workedExamples.title: Column addition with one carry
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Ones: 3 + 6 = 9, write 9. Tens: 2 + 9 = 11, write 1 and carry 1 hundred. Hundreds: 1 (carried) + 5 + 1 = 7, write 7. The answer is 719.

workedExamples.title: Column addition crossing into the thousands
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Ones: 8 + 4 = 12, write 2, carry 1. Tens: 1 + 3 + 9 = 13, write 3, carry 1. Hundreds: 1 + 7 + 2 = 10, write 0 and carry 1 thousand. The answer is 1,032. When the hundreds column reaches ten or more, you carry into a brand-new thousands place.

workedExamples.title: Column subtraction with borrowing
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Ones: 5 − 9 will not work, borrow 1 ten – 4 tens become 3, 5 ones become 15; 15 − 9 = 6. Tens: 3 − 5 will not work, borrow 1 hundred – 7 hundreds become 6, 3 tens become 13; 13 − 5 = 8. Hundreds: 6 − 2 = 4. The answer is 486.

workedExamples.title: Subtraction by counting up (number line)
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Start at 287. Jump to 300 (13). Jump to 500 (200). Jump to 563 (63). Add the jumps: 13 + 200 + 63 = 276.

workedExamples.title: A comparison word problem
workedExamples.prompt: Maryam is 143 cm tall. Her brother Idris is 178 cm tall. How much taller is Idris?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: This is a comparison, so we subtract: 178 − 143 = 35. Idris is 35 cm taller.

workedExamples.title: A multi-step problem
workedExamples.prompt: Sahra has 256 stickers. She gives 78 to a friend and later gets 45 new ones. How many does she have now?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Step 1: 256 − 78 = 178 after giving some away. Step 2: 178 + 45 = 223. Sahra now has 223 stickers.

workedExamples.title: Checking with the inverse
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Add the answer to the number taken away: 487 + 374 = 861. We land back on 861, so the subtraction is correct.

workedExamples.title: Subtract with the number line (counting up)
workedExamples.prompt: Subtract with the number line (counting up): a) 425 − 178 b) 936 − 547.

workedExamples.title: Check these additions with subtraction
workedExamples.prompt: Check these additions with subtraction: a) 523 + 196 = 719 b) 738 + 294 = 1,032.

workedExamples.title: Check these subtractions with addition
workedExamples.prompt: Check these subtractions with addition: a) 745 − 259 = 486 b) 904 − 567 = 337.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Find the missing number: □ + 285 = 632. Explain how the inverse operation helps.
workedExamples.solution: 1) □ = 632 − 285 = 347. Subtraction is the inverse of addition, so we take the known part away from the total.

activities.title: The Shopping Game
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and some stones or bottle caps as pretend shillings.
steps: Write a price list, for example: mango 125, book 450, ball 275, dates 190.
Take turns 'shopping' by choosing two or three items.
Add the prices with the column method to find the total cost.
Work out the change from 1,000 shillings by subtracting.
Check each answer with a different method or the inverse operation.

activities.title: Number Puzzles with Missing Numbers
steps: Write puzzles with a missing number, such as □ + 285 = 632 or 523 − □ = 355.
Solve each puzzle using inverse operations (add to undo a subtraction, subtract to undo an addition).
Check your answer by putting your number back into the puzzle.
Swap puzzles with a family member and solve each other's.
Challenge: make a two-step puzzle that uses both an addition and a subtraction.

activities.title: Daily Maths Journal
steps: Each day, find a real situation with adding or subtracting – shopping, counting animals, sharing dates.
Write it as a word problem in your journal.
Solve it using one of the strategies you have learned.
Check your answer with a different method.
Share your favourite problem with a family member or friend.

activities.title: Regrouping with Bundles
activities.materials: many small sticks, stones or beans, and string or rubber bands.
steps: Make bundles of ten and single ones to build a three-digit number, such as 156.
Build a second number, such as 78, and join the two piles to add them.
Whenever you have ten ones, tie them into a new ten – this is carrying with your hands.
Whenever you have ten tens, tie them into a hundred bundle.
Count the final bundles and ones, and compare with the column method answer.

activities.title: Three-Ways Investigation
steps: Choose any two three-digit numbers, for example 468 and 375.
Add them with the column method.
Add them again with the expanded form method.
Add them a third time with the number line method.
Check that all three answers agree, then write a sentence about which method you liked best and why.

activities.title: Change at the Market (Family Game)
activities.materials: your family and pretend money made from paper.
steps: One person is the shopkeeper and sets prices for a few items.
Another person buys something and pays with a larger note.
The shopkeeper works out the change by counting up from the price to the note.
Everyone checks the change by adding it back to the price.
Swap roles so everyone practises both adding and subtracting.

rules.title: Column Method Rule
rules.text: Line up ones under ones, tens under tens, hundreds under hundreds. Always start from the ones column and work left.

rules.title: Carrying Rule
rules.text: (Addition) When a column reaches 10 or more, write the ones digit and carry the rest. Add the carried digit into the next column on the left.

rules.title: Borrowing Rule
rules.text: (Subtraction) If the top digit is smaller, borrow 1 from the next column on the left. The digit you borrow from goes down by one; the current digit gains ten.

terms: A number split into hundreds + tens + ones
A rounded, rough answer used to check
The opposite operation that undoes the first
A problem needing more than one operation
commonMistakes: Carry when a column reaches 10 or more
Say 'plus the carried one' each column
If the top is smaller, borrow instead
Cross out and lower the digit you borrowed from
Check with estimation or the inverse

Subtract tens: 30 − 40 will not work, so borrow 100 from the hundreds – the 300 becomes 200 and the 30 becomes 130; now 130 − 40 = 90. selfAssessment: I can add three-digit numbers using the column method, the expanded form method, and the number line method.
I can subtract three-digit numbers using the same three methods.
I can use regrouping (carrying) in addition and regrouping (borrowing) in subtraction confidently.
I can use mental strategies – making tens, breaking apart, and compensation – for quicker sums.
I can solve word problems that involve joining, separating, part-part-whole and comparison.
I can solve multi-step problems that need more than one operation.
I can check answers using estimation, inverse operations, and a different method.
I can choose the best strategy for each addition or subtraction problem.
